https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
PEARL – Residents have until Feb. 27 to donate canned corn as part of the March of the Mayors Extra Table Feeds food drive in Pearl.

City officials said this is the final week for donations, with canned corn being collected to help support families in need throughout the community.

Organizers emphasized that every donation contributes to putting food on the table for local families. Residents are encouraged to purchase extra cans during routine grocery trips and drop them off at any City of Pearl location listed on the city’s promotional materials.

The deadline to donate is Thursday, Feb. 27.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: PEARL – Residents have until Feb. 27 to donate canned corn as part of the March of the Mayors Extra Table Feeds food drive in Pearl.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Final week to donate corn for March of the Mayors food drive in Pearl - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
QUITMAN — First State Bank has expanded its lending team in Quitman, Miss. with the addition of Stephanie Hurd as assistant vice president and branch manager as well as Lowery Skinner as consumer lender. The new hires reflect the bank’s continued commitment to business growth and community development in Clarke County.

Hurd brings extensive banking experience and strong community ties to her new role. Previously, she served in multiple positions at Magnolia State Bank in Heidelberg, Miss., including branch manager, loan officer, and loan assistant. A graduate of Quitman High School, she earned her Bachelor of Business Administration from Belhaven University and associate degrees from Pearl River Community College.

“I am honored to return to my hometown and work alongside our customers,” Hurd said. “I look forward to helping grow Quitman’s businesses and being a part of its bright future.”

Lowery Skinner, a native of Waynesboro, Miss., earned a Bachelor of Business Administration in finance from Mississippi State University’s College of Business. He is also a graduate of Jones College in Ellisville, Miss. Since joining First State Bank part-time in 2023, he has served as a loan operations assistant, credit analyst and management trainee. His experience across departments provides a strong foundation for serving consumer lending needs in the Quitman market.

“Stephanie and Lowery embody the core values of First State Bank,” said Darron Dodd, executive vice president of retail banking. “Their experience and dedication will play an important role in strengthening relationships and supporting economic growth throughout Clarke County.”

First State Bank’s Quitman branch will host an open house reception on Thursday, March 5, 2026, at 310 S. Archusa Ave. in Quitman. The event will celebrate the new additions to the team, and all community members are invited to attend from 12:00 p.m. to 2:00 p.m. for light hors d’oeuvres.

Founded in 1934, First State Bank remains committed to meeting the financial needs of area individuals and businesses, all while providing dedicated customer service. Headquartered in Waynesboro, Miss., the organization has locations in Buckatunna, Clara, Ellisville, Laurel, Leakesville, Lucedale, Gluckstadt, Meridian, Quitman and State Line.

For additional information on First State Bank, visit www.firststatebnk.bank .
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: QUITMAN — First State Bank has expanded its lending team in Quitman, Miss. with the addition of Stephanie Hurd as assistant vice president and branch manager as well as Lowery Skinner as consumer lender. The new hires reflect the bank’s continued commitment to business growth and community development in Clarke County.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: First State Bank Welcomes Hurd and Skinner to Quitman Branch - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
FULTON — An Itawamba County man was arrested Feb. 18 after deputies discovered a felony amount of methamphetamine and a firearm during a traffic stop on Highway 178, authorities said.

Deputies with the Itawamba County Sheriff’s Department stopped a vehicle in Fulton and identified the driver as Jerrick Scott Keys of Fulton.

During the stop, deputies located what they described as a felonious amount of methamphetamine and a firearm inside the vehicle. An agent with the North Mississippi Narcotics Unit responded to the scene and assisted with a search of the vehicle, where an additional bag of methamphetamine was found, officials said.

Keys was taken into custody and transported to the Itawamba County Jail. Deputies reported that as Keys exited the patrol vehicle, they observed more methamphetamine on the floorboard where he had been seated.

While Keys was being processed into the jail, detention staff located a metal container on his person that also contained methamphetamine, authorities said.

Keys was charged with possession of a felony amount of methamphetamine, enhanced with a firearm, and tampering with evidence. His bond was set at $60,000.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: FULTON — An Itawamba County man was arrested Feb. 18 after deputies discovered a felony amount of methamphetamine and a firearm during a traffic stop on Highway 178, authorities said.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Fulton Man Arrested After Deputies Find Meth, Gun During Traffic Stop - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
NEWTON — A GoFundMe campaign has been launched to support Newton restaurant owner Ronnie Cook after he was diagnosed with stage 4 pancreatic cancer and is unable to work during treatment.

Cook, owner of Cook’s BBQ & Seafood in Newton , has stepped away from the restaurant as he undergoes treatment, with family members saying the exhaustion caused by the illness has made it impossible for him to continue working.

Supporters say the diagnosis has brought both physical and financial challenges as Cook begins intensive cancer treatment.

“Fighting pancreatic cancer like this can be draining and can be extremely expensive,” organizers wrote on the fundraising page. “We would love to be able to take some of the burden off of him and his family during this time so they can focus on treatment and not stress about money.”

Cook is described by family and friends as a devoted son, brother, husband, father, grandfather and friend who has long served the Newton community through his restaurant.

Organizers said donations of any amount are appreciated and asked for prayers for Cook and his family as he battles the disease.

The GoFundMe page is accepting online donations to help cover medical and living expenses during his treatment.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: NEWTON — A GoFundMe campaign has been launched to support Newton restaurant owner Ronnie Cook after he was diagnosed with stage 4 pancreatic cancer and is unable to work during treatment.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: GoFundMe Launched for Newton Restaurant Owner Battling Cancer - Darkhorse Press

LAWRENCE COUNTY – A Lawrence County animal shelter called Paws of Love is asking for the community to help them with dry dog food.

The shelter said they are completely out of dry food and still have some wet food cans for the animals in their care.

They said they take any brand of dry dog food but love the dry kibble at Rushing Ag LLC.

If you can help with even one bag, it would make a huge difference to the rescue.

We all need to step up and care for the forgotten animals in the community.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: LAWRENCE COUNTY – A Lawrence County animal shelter called Paws of Love is asking for the community to help them with dry dog food.

Police said Melody Chandler was last seen leaving her residence in the area of Ingalls Avenue on Feb. 22, 2026.

Chandler is described as 5 feet 4 inches tall, weighing approximately 200 pounds, with purple or auburn-colored hair.

Anyone with information about her whereabouts is asked to contact the Pascagoula Police Department at 228-762-2211.

PEARL — The Pearl Police Department is asking for the public’s help in locating a missing teenager who was last seen Tuesday.

Police said Cameron D’undre Primus, 16, was last seen February 24 in Pearl around The Highlands Trailer Park.

Primus is described as a Black male, about 5 feet 5 inches tall and weighing approximately 160 pounds. He was last seen wearing a white T-shirt with red writing and black pants.

Anyone with information about his whereabouts is asked to contact the Pearl Police Department at 601-939-7000.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
The Mississippi Department of Education has failed for years to publish federally required evaluations of its afterschool programs, according to a new analysis from the Mississippi Office of the State Auditor.

The report found that millions of federal dollars continue to flow from MDE to nonprofits and school districts to operate afterschool programs, even though required statewide evaluations have not been made public since November 2022.

In fiscal year 2024, nonprofits and school districts analyzed in the report spent more than $11 million in Nita M. Lowey 21st Century Community Learning Centers grant funds to provide afterschool care to students.

Under federal requirements, subgrantees must collect and submit performance data to MDE. The department is then required to use that data to complete an annual statewide program evaluation and submit it to the federal government. According to the auditor’s office, MDE has not published the required report in more than two years.

“As we have seen on the news in Minnesota and here at home during the welfare scandal, making sure that these taxpayer funds are monitored is critical,” State Auditor Shad White said in a statement. “We will continue to highlight any weaknesses we see in the programs providing money for afterschool or childcare centers.”

The auditor’s office also highlighted additional concerns in the report. Analysts said some subgrantees may have violated state and federal regulations by hiring external evaluators who were not independent. In one instance, a grantee hired an individual who worked for the nonprofit arm of the organization being evaluated, creating a potential conflict of interest.

The report also noted that attendance data for the centers grouped student participation in multi-day and multi-hour increments. As a result, a student who attended one day or only a few hours each month could be counted in the same attendance category as a student with perfect attendance.

The report is the second in a series examining taxpayer-funded childcare programs in Mississippi.

The full report is available on the State Auditor’s website under the “Reports” tab by searching “afterschool.”
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: The Mississippi Department of Education has failed for years to publish federally required evaluations of its afterschool programs, according to a new analysis from the Mississippi Office of the State Auditor.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: State auditor says MDE failed to publish required evaluations of afterschool programs - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
SARDIS – The Tunica Humane Society says it has rescued a dog that had been reported at a funeral home in Sardis and is now asking the public for donations as the animal receives emergency veterinary care.

In a social media post, the organization said the dog, now named “Beacon,” was rescued after volunteers spent hours trying to get him into a trap. The humane society thanked two men it described as “boots on the ground” in Sardis for helping with the rescue.

The organization said Beacon’s condition was more serious than expected and asked supporters to pray for his recovery.

In an update, the Tunica Humane Society said Beacon is currently at a veterinarian receiving emergency treatment and that officials are optimistic about his future, though they said he faces a long and costly recovery.

According to the organization, Beacon has severe mange with secondary infection, along with heartworms and internal parasites. The humane society said he is also anemic.

The group said it is committed to providing the senior dog with ongoing care and is accepting donations to help cover medical expenses. Donations can be made through the Tunica Humane Society’s online payment options listed on its social media pages, or by mailing checks to P.O. Box 236, Tunica, MS 38676.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: SARDIS – The Tunica Humane Society says it has rescued a dog that had been reported at a funeral home in Sardis and is now asking the public for donations as the animal receives emergency veterinary care.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Dog with severe mange rescued outside funeral home in Sardis - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard

NORTH FORREST – Firefighters with the North Forrest Volunteer Fire Department responded to two mutual aid structure fires Tuesday evening, according to the department.

At approximately 6 p.m. on Feb. 24, units were dispatched to the area of Morriston Road and Shady Oak Lane in the Macedonia Volunteer Fire Department’s jurisdiction for a reported structure fire.

Upon arrival, crews reported heavy fire coming from a mobile home. Firefighters assisted with a defensive attack and remained on scene for nearly two hours before the fire was brought under control. No injuries were reported.

Later, at approximately 10 p.m., crews responded to the 100 block of Creek Lane in the Rawls Springs community for another reported structure fire.

Firefighters arrived to find a single-story, wood-framed house with fire venting through the roof. Crews made an aggressive interior attack and quickly brought the fire under control, officials said.

No injuries were reported in either incident.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: NORTH FORREST – Firefighters with the North Forrest Volunteer Fire Department responded to two mutual aid structure fires Tuesday evening, according to the department.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Firefighters assist Macedonia VFD, later respond to Rawls Springs fire - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
HATTIESBURG – Four juvenile spider monkeys are now calling the Hattiesburg Zoo home after being confiscated in an illegal pet trade smuggling attempt, according to zoo officials.

The monkeys were discovered by U.S. Fish and Wildlife Services at the Mexico-United States border, where they had been hidden inside lunch boxes. The animals were confiscated under the Lacey Act, a federal law that prohibits the import, export, transport, sale or purchase of wildlife taken in violation of federal, state, tribal or foreign laws.

The Hattiesburg Zoo said it was contacted by the Association of Zoos and Aquariums Wildlife Trafficking Alliance and the Species Survival Program and was asked to permanently house and care for the primates.

“The pet trade is very dangerous and often results in the death of the mothers of the primates, which are purposefully or accidentally killed by traffickers attempting to separate and capture their babies,” said Kristen Moore, director of wildlife for the Hattiesburg Zoo. Moore said more than 2,000 primates are attempted to be smuggled into the United States each year.

Moore said spider monkeys live in large social groups and form strong family bonds that cannot be replicated in a residential setting. She added that the animals require specialized diets, habitats and care.

Spider monkeys are native to the tropical forests of Central and South America, ranging from southern Mexico to Brazil. In the wild, they live high in the rainforest canopy and primarily eat fruit, along with leaves, flowers and insects.

Jeremy Cumpton, the zoo’s director of conservation and education, said educating guests about the dangers of the illegal pet trade is part of the zoo’s mission.

“While we are sad about how these monkeys came to call the Hattiesburg Zoo home, we are happy that our animal care and veterinary teams will be able to care for them,” Cumpton said.

The four juveniles, two males and two females, completed a quarantine period and are now being introduced to their outdoor habitat alongside Rico, a senior adult male spider monkey who has lived at the zoo for several years.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: HATTIESBURG – Four juvenile spider monkeys are now calling the Hattiesburg Zoo home after being confiscated in an illegal pet trade smuggling attempt, according to zoo officials.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Hattiesburg Zoo houses four spider monkeys confiscated in illegal pet trade smuggled in lunchboxes - Darkhorse Press

JACKSON — More than 1,000 Jackson Public Schools students and their families gathered Feb. 21 for “Legacy on the Block: Jackson’s Day of Cultural Legacy Block Party” at the Two Mississippi Museums, according to Jackson Public Schools.

The event featured activities for students and families, including screenings of “Xavier Riddle and the Secret Museum” from PBS Kids, trackless train rides, inflatables, T-shirt making and food from local vendors. Students also received vouchers for free children’s meals from participating food trucks.

District officials said the event was part of a national effort focused on teaching, protecting and celebrating Black history across generations. Organizers said the goal was to invest in cultural spaces and transform museums into interactive learning environments where students can connect with history.

Jackson Public Schools partnered with Fahrenheit Creative Group and Color of Change to host the event.

City of Jackson and Hinds County officials, along with members of the Mississippi Legislative Black Caucus, were also acknowledged for their support, according to the district.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON — More than 1,000 Jackson Public Schools students and their families gathered Feb. 21 for “Legacy on the Block: Jackson’s Day of Cultural Legacy Block Party” at the Two Mississippi Museums, according to Jackson Public Schools.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Jackson Public Schools hosts Black History Month block party at Two Mississippi Museums - Darkhorse Press

JACKSON – A man was injured in a house fire in Jackson, according to Chief Felton.

The fire broke out at 641 Daniel Circle and fire units responded around 8:09 a.m.

When firefighters arrived on scene, they said they saw smoke and flames coming from the structure.

Fire units said there was a person standing outside of the structure when they arrived.

The occupant had minor burns to both hands and was transported to a local hospital and is expected to make a full recovery.

This fire was caused as as a result of combustibles being too close to a heater.

The cause of the fire was deemed accidental.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Special to Darkhorse Press
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
National Consumer Protection Week is the first week of March.

By Mississippi Insurance Commissioner Mike Chaney Insurance fraud is not a victimless crime. It’s been around for as long as insurance, and it costs businesses billions of dollars each year – an expense that is passed on to consumers in the form of higher premiums. Although the overwhelming majority of insurance agents and companies are honest and reputable, there are some bad actors.

My office deals with common scenarios, including scammers offering fake policies at costs that are significantly lower than competitors’ prices; legitimate companies selling non-insurance products marketed to look like insurance; and dishonest insurance agents “pocketing” a policyholder’s premium payment, instead of sending it on to the insurance company, as they should, leaving the consumer without coverage.

In 2025, the Mississippi Insurance Department (MID) Investigations Division investigated over 800 complaints. Over 400 of those were fraud-related. MID recovered more than $1.1 million for consumers following our investigations.

Companies and agents aren’t the only ones guilty: dishonest contractors also run scams. After a disaster, fraudsters and scam artists often arrive quickly. Sometimes, they even come from out of town to take advantage of people. Contact your insurance company as soon as possible to file a claim. Your insurer will verify the repairs that need to be made. Make sure the contractor you hire for home repairs is legitimate. Search for the company online and read reviews. Find out if there are complaints against the contractor with the Better Business Bureau or the Attorney General’s office. Finally, verify that the contractor is licensed by the state Board of Contractors.

Consumers can also be guilty of insurance fraud. The most common forms of policyholder fraud relate to auto insurance and workers’ compensation. Deliberately staging an accident, exaggerating a legitimate claim, or knowingly providing false information on an application are all examples of insurance fraud, which is a crime.

According to the Coalition Against Insurance Fraud (CAIF), 3 of 4 insurance companies believe insurance fraud is growing, signaling a concern within the industry. A recent report from CAIF shows that $308.6B is stolen every year.

The National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) recommends following these tips to protect yourself from fraud:

If you are a victim of insurance fraud or you are aware of fraudulent activity, call my office at 601-359-3569 or file a complaint online at www.mid.ms.gov . We work with consumers on these issues every day. We’re here to help you.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: National Consumer Protection Week is the first week of March.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: OP-ED: Insurance Fraud Affects Everyone - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
RANKIN COUNTY – The Rankin County Sheriff’s Office has arrested a suspect in connection with a burglary at a fish house on Dixie Road in the Florence area.

Deputies received a report of a commercial burglary on Feb. 24 after employees discovered cash missing from the register when they opened the business for the day.

A review of security footage showed a male suspect wearing a black hoodie walking near the rear of the restaurant the previous night, according to the sheriff’s office. Investigators said the suspect went to the electrical box and turned off the breaker, cutting power to the restaurant. Additional surveillance cameras in the area also captured images of the suspect.

Deputies began investigating and identified and arrested the suspect in less than 24 hours.

The suspect was identified as Scott Dennis Roberts of Rankin County. He was booked into the Rankin County Jail on a charge of burglary.

The Rankin County District Attorney’s Office will bring Roberts before County Court Judge David Morrow for an initial appearance.

Sheriff Bryan Bailey thanked the deputies involved in the case and said the department will continue working to keep Rankin County safe.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: RANKIN COUNTY – The Rankin County Sheriff’s Office has arrested a suspect in connection with a burglary at a fish house on Dixie Road in the Florence area.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Rankin County man arrested in fish house burglary - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel

FLOWOOD — The City of Flowood will relaunch its pop-up food series, The Lot at Liberty, this March at Liberty Park, offering weekly gatherings featuring local food vendors in an open-air setting.

The series will take place every Thursday from 5 to 8 p.m. throughout March. Admission is free, and no tickets are required.

The Lot at Liberty will feature a curated mix of local vendors serving a variety of savory dishes and sweet treats. Organizers said the event is designed to provide an easy dinner option for sports families, working professionals and others looking for a relaxed evening out.

Set among Liberty Park’s ballfields, pickleball courts and playgrounds, the series aims to blend community and convenience in a family-friendly atmosphere. City officials described the event as a casual pop-up experience where guests can gather outdoors and enjoy a rotating lineup of food vendors each week.

The city encourages residents to follow @visitflowoodms on Facebook, Instagram and TikTok for updates on the weekly vendor lineup.

More information is available at www.visitflowood.com/the-lot-at-liberty or by contacting the Flowood Visitor Center at 601-932-8007 or [email protected] .
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: FLOWOOD — The City of Flowood will relaunch its pop-up food series, The Lot at Liberty, this March at Liberty Park, offering weekly gatherings featuring local food vendors in an open-air setting.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: The Lot at Liberty Returns to Flowood’s Liberty Park for March Food Series - Darkhorse Press

JACKSON – The University of Mississippi Medical Center said Wednesday it is making “significant progress” in responding to a cyberattack and restoring systems, while continuing to cancel regularly scheduled clinic appointments and elective procedures through Friday.

In a Feb. 25 update, UMMC said all hospitals and emergency departments in Jackson, Madison County, Holmes County and Grenada remain open.

UMMC said regularly scheduled clinic appointments and elective procedures will be canceled through Friday, with canceled appointments to be rescheduled.

Patients with time-sensitive needs are encouraged to call the automated UMMC Triage Line at 601-815-0000. UMMC said patients who contact the triage line and require urgent care will be contacted directly to schedule a clinic visit from Wednesday through Friday.

Hospital officials said they are hopeful elective procedures and regular clinic operations could resume as early as Monday, though they said the timeline should become clearer over the next couple of days.

UMMC apologized for the inconvenience.

UMMC closes clinics Wednesday, activates triage phone line
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON – The University of Mississippi Medical Center said Wednesday it is making “significant progress” in responding to a cyberattack and restoring systems, while continuing to cancel regularly scheduled clinic appointments and elective procedures through Friday.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: UMMC reports “significant progress” in cyberattack recovery - Darkhorse Press

BAY ST. LOUIS — Bay St. Louis Police Department is asking for the public’s help identifying a person accused of using false pretenses at a business along U.S. 90 last month.

The incident occurred Jan. 29 at approximately 5:58 p.m. at a business located on Highway 90 in Bay St. Louis, according to police.

Authorities said the individual was wearing a black sweatshirt, black sweatpants, brown beaver slippers and a navy-blue visor at the time of the incident.

Investigators have not released additional details about the alleged offense but are urging anyone who recognizes the individual to come forward.

Tips can be submitted through Crime Stoppers by downloading the P3 Tips app or by calling **TIPS. To be eligible for a reward, information must be submitted directly through Crime Stoppers, police said.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: BAY ST. LOUIS — Bay St. Louis Police Department is asking for the public’s help identifying a person accused of using false pretenses at a business along U.S. 90 last month.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
JACKSON — Jackson Public Schools is highlighting the legacy of James Roosevelt Johnson, who in 1971 became the first African American elected to the Jackson Public School Board, then known as the Jackson Municipal Separate School District.

According to the district, Johnson’s election marked a pivotal moment as the school system continued moving from segregation toward greater equity and representation in the years following the U.S. Supreme Court’s decision in Brown v. Board of Education.

Johnson later became the first Black chairman of the school board in 1980.

District officials said Johnson’s leadership helped guide Jackson Public Schools through integration efforts during the 1960s and 1970s.

Beyond his service to the school district, Johnson was active in Mississippi politics. He ran for the Mississippi State Senate and later for the U.S. House of Representatives in 1978. He also served as president of the Mississippi Junior Chamber of Commerce, becoming the youngest person in the organization’s history to hold the position.

Johnson’s legacy is preserved in the James R. Johnson Papers, which are housed in the Mississippi Digital Library and document his leadership and service to education and the Jackson community.

Jackson Public Schools said Johnson’s contributions continue to inspire progress and opportunity for students across the district.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON — Jackson Public Schools is highlighting the legacy of James Roosevelt Johnson, who in 1971 became the first African American elected to the Jackson Public School Board, then known as the Jackson Municipal Separate School District.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Jackson Public Schools honors legacy of James Roosevelt Johnson during Black History Month - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
CORINTH- The Corinth-Alcorn Animal Shelter is hoping to find a permanent home for Loraine, a 1-and-a-half-year-old Mastiff and Shepherd mix who has been waiting for adoption since July.

Shelter officials said Loraine arrived with five other dogs after their owner entered assisted living. While the other dogs have since been adopted, Loraine is still at the shelter.

Loraine weighs about 72 pounds and is fully vaccinated, spayed, heartworm negative and on monthly preventatives, according to the shelter. Staff said she is dog friendly, does not mind cats and is affectionate with people.

Shelter officials described Loraine as having a gentle and loyal personality and said she has spent months waiting as other animals found homes.

Those interested in adopting Loraine can visit the Corinth-Alcorn Animal Shelter or view adoption information online at corinthalcornanimalshelter.org/adopt-2.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: CORINTH- The Corinth-Alcorn Animal Shelter is hoping to find a permanent home for Loraine, a 1-and-a-half-year-old Mastiff and Shepherd mix who has been waiting for adoption since July.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: DARKHORSE DOGS: Loraine, the cutest mastiff shepherd mix wants to slobber on your couch - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
JACKSON — A long-debated flood control project for the Pearl River is moving forward after the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers released its final Environmental Impact Statement, advancing a compromise plan supporters say will protect thousands of homes and businesses while opening the door to riverfront redevelopment in the capital region.

Officials from the Rankin-Hinds Pearl River Flood Control District including Jackson, Flowood, Pearl held a press conference Thursday morning to announce that the project will move forward.

The plan, known as “Alternative D1,” has the backing of the Pearl River Revitalization Coalition and was advanced in coordination with the Rankin-Hinds Pearl River Flood and Drainage Control District. Officials from the City of Jackson joined leaders from Pearl, Flowood and other communities to announce the milestone.

Supporters say the project is aimed at addressing decades of flooding along the Pearl River, which inundated neighborhoods in 2020 and again in 2022, damaging homes, businesses, public infrastructure and water treatment systems across the Jackson metro area.

Alternative D1 focuses on improving flood conveyance by widening sections of the river, constructing new levees to protect vulnerable neighborhoods and installing a modern, controllable weir that includes a fish passage to balance water management with ecosystem needs.

Beyond flood protection, the plan includes creating river-adjacent parks, trails and gathering spaces designed to reconnect residents with the river. Green infrastructure components call for constructed wetlands to manage stormwater, filter pollutants, reduce sediment buildup and improve water quality.

Jeff Rent, president and CEO of the Greater Jackson Chamber Partnership, pointed out that other municipalities have embraced their urban waterfronts and built out their property values and tax bases.

The Pearl River was channelized by the Corps in the 1960s when a levee system was built to protect Jackson and Rankin County. Today, officials say the river’s altered condition, coupled with aging water and sewer infrastructure, sewage leaks and litter, presents environmental and public safety challenges. Limited public access to the river has further hindered restoration efforts.

Coalition leaders said Alternative D1 is smaller than previous proposals, minimizing impacts to existing wetlands, including areas where bald cypress trees are thriving. If approved, the group plans to hold design charrettes to develop features such as raised walkways through wetlands to protect wildlife while providing public access and educational opportunities.

Officials said the project represents a significant step toward protecting residents, strengthening regional stability and supporting long-term economic growth. The levee system, they said, could eventually be reimagined as a corridor for parks and trail systems that integrate recreation, connectivity and environmental stewardship.

While the project’s first priority is flood control, supporters say the broader vision includes ecological restoration, public education and new green spaces aimed at improving quality of life and building long-term resilience for communities along the Pearl River basin.

Federal, state and local partners have not yet announced a clear construction timeline, but authorities say it could take up to three years once it begins in late 2026 or early 2027, depending on financing.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON — A long-debated flood control project for the Pearl River is moving forward after the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers released its final Environmental Impact Statement, advancing a compromise plan supporters say will protect thousands of homes and businesses while opening the door to riverfront redevelopment in the capital region.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Federal flood control plan advances for Pearl River in Jackson metro - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
RANKIN COUNTY – Daniel and Sherry Finnegan, owners and employees of Finnegan Fire Safety Equipment, pleaded guilty Tuesday in Rankin County, according to the Mississippi Office of the State Fire Marshal.

Officials said Daniel Finnegan was sentenced to 20 years, with seven years to serve. Sherry Finnegan was sentenced to five years, with four years suspended and one year to serve.

The state fire marshal’s office said the plea agreements cover charges filed in both Rankin and Madison counties. Across the two counties, officials said 12 charges were filed against Daniel Finnegan and 12 against Sherry Finnegan stemming from six separate cases.

Authorities said the Finnegans were originally charged with 41 counts each of false pretense, a felony, along with 37 counts each of violations of the Mississippi Fire Prevention Code, which are misdemeanors.

Despite the pleas entered in Rankin and Madison counties, officials said the Finnegans still face additional charges, including 31 Mississippi Fire Prevention Code violations and 37 false pretense charges that remain pending. The investigation involves 13 counties, and charges remain active in 11 counties, according to the fire marshal’s office.

Based on documentation obtained and verified during the investigation, officials said confirmed victims paid the Finnegans a total of $343,162.69. The fire marshal’s office said the amount does not reflect the full financial impact because many victims also paid additional costs to correct deficient installations and bring fire protection systems into compliance.

Officials said restitution ordered for Daniel Finnegan totals $67,853.95, and restitution ordered for Sherry Finnegan totals $20,000.

The Mississippi Office of the State Fire Marshal said it will continue to monitor remaining cases in other counties and provide updates as more information becomes available.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: RANKIN COUNTY – Daniel and Sherry Finnegan, owners and employees of Finnegan Fire Safety Equipment, pleaded guilty Tuesday in Rankin County, according to the Mississippi Office of the State Fire Marshal.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Finnegan Fire Safety owners sentenced after guilty pleas in Rankin County - Darkhorse Press

RIDGELAND — The City of Ridgeland will begin work on the Lake Harbour Drive overlay project on Monday, March 2. Construction will take place along Lake Harbour Drive between Christine Drive and Northpark Drive.

The project is expected to take approximately six months to complete. Throughout construction, at least one lane of traffic will remain open at all times to help minimize disruptions. This overlay project represents an important infrastructure investment aimed at improving roadway conditions and ensuring the long-term safety and reliability of one of Ridgeland’s primary corridors.

Warning signs and detour signage will be clearly posted to assist drivers. The City will continue to provide updates as work progresses and appreciates the public’s patience and cooperation during construction.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: RIDGELAND — The City of Ridgeland will begin work on the Lake Harbour Drive overlay project on Monday, March 2. Construction will take place along Lake Harbour Drive between Christine Drive and Northpark Drive.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Lake Harbour Drive overlay begins March 2 in Ridgeland - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
STARKVILLE — British Consul General to the Southeast U.S. Rachel Galloway visited Mississippi State today for a discussion with university President Mark E. Keenum on leadership and shared U.K.-American cultural and education connections, including the highly coveted Marshall Scholarship.

Their meeting followed a day of tours and talks across campus for the Consulate who viewed MSU’s Ulysses S. Grant Presidential Library, Raspet Flight Research Laboratory and the Advanced Composites Institute, ending the day in talks with several of the state’s aerospace and defense company leaders.

Galloway also took part in a Shackouls Honors College Lunch and Learn where she reminded students and faculty of her country’s Marshall Scholarship opportunity. The prestigious program pays for up to 50 select American graduate students to study at a U.K. university each year. MSU 2024 biological sciences graduate Madison Brode of Memphis, Tennessee, was the university’s first Marshall recipient in 2023. The honors college annually provides pathways for students to excel in global study, leadership and service, and the university’s Office of Prestigious External Scholarships supports MSU students applying for nationally and internationally competitive scholarships and fellowships.

A former ambassador to the Republic of North Macedonia, Galloway began serving as Consul General in Atlanta, Georgia, in 2022. She is one of seven U.K. consuls general in the U.S., not including the country’s embassy in Washington, D.C.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: STARKVILLE — British Consul General to the Southeast U.S. Rachel Galloway visited Mississippi State today for a discussion with university President Mark E. Keenum on leadership and shared U.K.-American cultural and education connections, including the highly coveted Marshall Scholarship.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Mississippi State welcomes British Consul General to campus - Darkhorse Press

A woman from Mississippi was killed Tuesday night in a crash involving a train in Shelby County, according to the Shelby County Sheriff’s Office.

WBRC reported a large law enforcement presence near the railroad crossing on County Road 433 in Chelsea shortly before 9 p.m. Tuesday.

The Shelby County Coroner’s Office identified the woman as 31-year-old Michelle A. Manning, who had been living in Birmingham recently, according to WBRC.

The Shelby County Sheriff’s Office said the crossing would be closed “for an extended period of time,” and later reported it reopened by about 10:30 p.m., according to WBRC.

Deputies urged drivers to avoid the area and take alternate routes while the scene was secured.

WBRC reported the Alabama Law Enforcement Agency is investigating the crash.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: A woman from Mississippi was killed Tuesday night in a crash involving a train in Shelby County, according to the Shelby County Sheriff’s Office.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Mississippi woman killed in train crash at Shelby County crossing - Darkhorse Press

TUPELO – A motorcyclist was killed in a traffic crash Tuesday on North Gloster Street, according to the Tupelo Police Department.

Police said officers responded to a fatal crash involving a vehicle and a motorcycle near the Mississippi Department of Transportation offices on North Gloster Street.

Authorities initially shut down North Gloster Street due to the heavy police presence and investigation at the scene. In an update, police said the roadway has since reopened and the scene has been cleared.

No additional details about the victim or the circumstances of the crash were immediately released. Police said more information will be provided as it becomes available.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: TUPELO – A motorcyclist was killed in a traffic crash Tuesday on North Gloster Street, according to the Tupelo Police Department.

NATCHEZ — The Adams County Sheriff’s Office said a Natchez woman has been arrested in connection with a credit card fraud investigation.

According to the sheriff’s office, a deputy conducted a traffic stop at approximately 9:24 a.m. Feb. 25 on West Stiers Lane. During the stop, authorities said it was determined the driver, 29-year-old Mahalia Bruce of Natchez, had an outstanding warrant issued by the Adams County Justice Court.

The warrant stems from an incident reported Feb. 24 in which Bruce is accused of unlawfully using a credit card number without the cardholder’s consent to make purchases at Walmart and Sonic in Adams County, authorities said. The charge listed is fraud involving a credit card.

Bruce was taken into custody without incident and transported to the Adams County Jail, the sheriff’s office said. She was booked on charges of credit card fraud and embezzlement involving agents, trustees, clerks or other persons in a position of trust. She is being held pending an initial appearance before a judge.

The sheriff’s office noted that all individuals are presumed innocent unless proven guilty in a court of law.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: NATCHEZ — The Adams County Sheriff’s Office said a Natchez woman has been arrested in connection with a credit card fraud investigation.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Natchez woman arrested in Adams County credit card fraud investigation - Darkhorse Press

PASCAGOULA — Law enforcement is investigating a threat discovered Thursday morning at Ingalls Shipbuilding on the Mississippi Coast, according to WLOX-TV.

WLOX reported that someone found a threatening note along with a bullet inside a bathroom at the shipyard.

Authorities have not released the contents of the note.

Police said the investigation is ongoing, and no arrests have been announced.

Ingalls Shipbuilding told WLOX that operations at the facility are continuing as normal.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
SOUTHWEST MISSISSIPPI — Several southwest Mississippi counties are under burn bans as of Thursday morning, restricting all outdoor burning amid ongoing dry conditions.

According to county officials and the Mississippi Forestry Commission, burn bans are currently in effect for the following counties:

Scattered light rain showers could move across the region Thursday morning, with higher rain and thunderstorm chances expected late Thursday afternoon into the evening as a cold front pushes through the state. Isolated severe storms capable of producing hail up to quarter size are possible across parts of north and central Mississippi.

While rainfall in the forecast could improve conditions, officials caution that it may not be enough to immediately lift burn bans. Residents are urged to check the current status of their county’s burn ban before conducting any outdoor burning.

A burn ban is a restriction on outdoor burning during drought or heightened wildfire conditions. County boards of supervisors typically request burn bans, and the Mississippi Forestry Commission approves them. All burn bans expire at midnight on the stated end date unless extended.

Under a burn ban, no outdoor burning of any kind is allowed. The local sheriff’s department enforces burn bans, and anyone who knowingly and willfully violates a ban is guilty of a misdemeanor. Violators may face a fine of not less than $100 and not more than $500 under Section 49-19-351 of the Mississippi Code of 1972, as amended.

Items not allowed during a burn ban include campfires, bonfires, fire pits, fire rings, burn barrels, debris burning and field burning. Even small fires can pose a risk, as wind can carry embers up to one-half mile away and ignite spot fires.

Certain activities may be exempt if specifically listed in the county’s burn ban order. Possible exemptions can include certified burn managers, county fire services, commercial contractors using heavy construction equipment in compliance with Mississippi Department of Environmental Quality regulations, agricultural field burns and other designated exceptions.

Propane or gas grills and heaters and charcoal grills are generally allowed, provided they are used according to manufacturer instructions, kept away from combustible materials and never left unattended. Officials stress that charcoal briquettes should be allowed to cool completely and doused with water before being disposed of in a metal container. Ashes should be cold to the touch before disposal.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: SOUTHWEST MISSISSIPPI — Several southwest Mississippi counties are under burn bans as of Thursday morning, restricting all outdoor burning amid ongoing dry conditions.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Southwest Mississippi counties under burn ban as possible light rain moves in - Darkhorse Press

JACKSON — Three people were arrested Wednesday night after a driver fled from Capitol Police officers and crashed following a brief pursuit in Jackson.

Officers attempted to initiate a traffic stop at approximately 8:33 p.m. near John R. Lynch Street and Arbor Vista Boulevard on Feb. 25, according to authorities. The driver failed to stop, leading officers on a short pursuit that ended when the vehicle wrecked at the intersection of Grand Avenue and West Capitol Street.

Police said three individuals inside the vehicle were taken into custody at the scene without further incident.

The driver, 26-year-old Destiney Parks, was charged with felony fleeing.

A passenger, 31-year-old Keeunna Welch, was charged with disorderly conduct for failure to comply with lawful commands.

A second passenger, 35-year-old Jeremy Crump, was found to have an active warrant from the Ridgeland Police Department and was released to that agency’s custody.

No additional information was immediately released.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON — Three people were arrested Wednesday night after a driver fled from Capitol Police officers and crashed following a brief pursuit in Jackson.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Three arrested after brief pursuit ends in crash in Jackson - Darkhorse Press

TUPELO- On February 18, Tupelo police officers responded to the North Mississippi Medical Center in reference to a previous sexual assault that allegedly happened at a Tupelo home.

The victim gave the officers the alleged suspect’s information.

On February 24, after further investigation, 34-year-old Juan Jose Cabral, from Tupelo, was arrested and charged with one count of Sexual Battery and one count of Rape.

On February 27, Tupelo Municipal Court Judge Jay Weird ordered Cabral to be held on a $40,000 bond. These charges will be presented to the Lee County Grand Jury.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.author: Special to Darkhorse Press
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.content_html:
By Kelli Sharpe Jackson State University

JACKSON — Today, in celebration of Black History Month and in anticipation of the worldwide theatrical release of “MICHAEL,” Jackson State University’s Sonic Boom of the South steps into the national spotlight once again with a powerful performance of Michael Jackson’s classic hit “Don’t Stop Til You Get Enough,” blending JSU’s rich heritage with the pop icon’s unmistakable groove.

“Michael Jackson’s global impact on music and culture is undeniable,” said Jackson State University Interim President Denise Jones Gregory, Ph.D. “Featuring the Sonic Boom of the South in this national campaign reflects the level of excellence, discipline and tradition that define Jackson State University and HBCUs nationwide. We are proud to see our students represent that legacy on a national stage.”

The Sonic Boom, celebrated as the summa cum laude of bands, is no stranger to the limelight. However, being a part of this initiative has a special meaning for senior Desi Jenkins, a music education major and lead drum major of the “Jackson 5”, fondly known as the J5.

“Throughout my entire career as a student, I’ve performed Michael’s selections and dance routines, with the most recent being our Homecoming 2024 halftime show,” Jenkins said. “I choreographed and facilitated my entire ‘Thriller’ segment for the show. Michael is and will always be my favorite artist, humanitarian and inspiration. Being part of the campaign of this film is extremely full-circle and everything greater than special to me. I am absolutely humbled and honored!”

“It is always a tremendous honor to represent our institution and band program on a national stage,” Roderick Little, Ph.D., band director of the Sonic Boom added. “This opportunity and iconic moment carries even greater significance as we celebrate the legacy of Michael Jackson, arguably one of the greatest entertainers of all time. Most importantly, this experience represents a milestone that our students will cherish for a lifetime. It is an achievement they will proudly look back on and say they were part of something truly special.”

“MICHAEL CELEBRATES: Legacy, Artistry, Culture,” pays tribute to Jackson’s legacy through powerful performances rooted in culture, tradition and pride. The initiative reflects the shared, enduring relationship between the HBCU community and Michael’s life and music.

“For Michael’s fans everywhere, his legacy of performance and artistry is enduring. That legacy lives powerfully within HBCU communities, where music, movement and excellence have long been expressions of culture, pride and identity,” said Briana McElroy, head of digital marketing for the Lionsgate Motion Picture Group. “This initiative is deeply personal to me. I grew up listening to these bands that represent Black excellence, community and creativity. Partnering with these HBCUs is about honoring that legacy while creating space for the next generation to lead, perform and inspire.”

You can see The Sonic Boom’s performance now on all JSU social media platforms and across the “MICHAEL” movie socials. “MICHAEL” releases in theaters everywhere April 24, 2026.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.summary: By Kelli Sharpe Jackson State University
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.title: JSU’s Sonic Boom proudly joins “MICHAEL CELEBRATES: Legacy, Artistry, Culture” - Darkhorse Press
